---
title: "CB3 Equity Report — Environmental Conditions Maps"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: cb3
---

## Purpose

This notebook reads the clean Environmental Conditions tract table and maps four
representative dimensions:

1. Rodent inspections (confirmed activity): `rodent_active_inspections`
2. Street and sidewalk cleanliness: `avg_acceptable_streets_pct`, `avg_acceptable_sidewalks_pct`
3. Street tree health: individual living street trees (2015 TreesCount census)
   colored by health status (Good/Fair/Poor)
4. Hurricane evaluation zones: these are overlaid with NYCHA housing and subsidized units at risk
5. EJ Area designation: `ej_area_flag` (shown as a hatched overlay on every map)
6. Fine particulate matter (PM2.5): NYCCAS modeled annual average, Dec 2023-Dec 2024,
   shown as its native 300m grid cells rather than a tract statistic

Every numeric metric is presented as a bubble layer sized by magnitude over
selectable demographic choropleth base layers. Rodent inspections are point
events with native building coordinates, so their bubbles plot at each
building's own address. Street and sidewalk cleanliness are reported at the
DSNY cleaning section level, not census tract, so their bubbles sit at each
section's centroid instead. Street tree health has no magnitude to size by —
each tree is plotted at its own coordinates as a fixed-size marker colored by
category instead of a bubble. Map HTML files are written to `maps/environment/`.

```{python}
# Import packages for tract data, geometry, classification, and interactive maps.
from pathlib import Path
import sys
import yaml

import folium
import geopandas as gpd
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import IFrame

# Ensure the reusable scripts package imports whether this QMD is rendered from
# the project root or from scripts/.
for candidate in [Path.cwd(), *Path.cwd().parents]:
    if (candidate / "src" / "map_utils.py").exists():
        sys.path.insert(0, str(candidate))
        break

from src.cb3_utils import add_polygon_centroids
from src.map_utils import (
    add_bubble_layer,
    add_categorical_legend,
    add_categorical_point_layer,
    add_demographic_backdrop_layers,
    add_map_title,
    add_zero_value_legend,
    build_metric_bubble_map,
    find_project_dir,
    make_base_map,
)
```

```{python}
# Define project inputs and output locations. Resolve the project root so the
# notebook works whether it is rendered from the root folder or from scripts/.
PROJECT_DIR = find_project_dir()
CLEAN_DATA_PATH = PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "environment_tract.csv"
SECTIONS_DATA_PATH = PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "environment_sections.csv"
BASELINE_PROFILE_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "cb3_tract_baseline_profile_clean.csv"
)
RODENT_INSPECTION_POINTS_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "environment_rodent_inspection_points.csv"
)
TREE_POINTS_PATH = PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "environment_tree_points.csv"
NYCHA_BUILDING_POINTS_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "environment_nycha_building_points.csv"
)
SUBSIDIZED_POINTS_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "housing_and_affordability_subsidized_points.csv"
)
TRACT_GEOMETRY_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "raw" / "Geography" / "2020_Census_Tracts_20260505.geojson"
)
SECTIONS_GEOMETRY_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "raw" / "Geography" / "DSNY_Sections_20260630.geojson"
)
EVAC_ZONES_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "cb3_hurricane_evacuation_zones.geojson"
)
PM25_GRID_PATH = PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "environment_pm25_grid.geojson"
MAP_OUTPUT_DIR = PROJECT_DIR / "maps" / "environment"
MAP_OUTPUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

with open(PROJECT_DIR / "docs" / "metric_metadata.yml") as f:
    ALL_METRICS = yaml.safe_load(f)


def show_map(output_path, height=650):
    """Display a saved map via a lightweight <iframe src=...> reference
    instead of returning the Folium object directly. Returning the object
    triggers Folium's _repr_html_, which inlines the ENTIRE map document as
    one escaped srcdoc attribute — for point-heavy maps (e.g. street tree
    health, ~6MB with 4,709 markers) that overflows Quarto's markdown
    formatter when it tries to process the cell output. Quarto always
    renders this .qmd's HTML report alongside the .qmd itself
    (scripts/environment/), so the relative path back up to maps/environment/
    is fixed regardless of the working directory execution used.
    """
    return IFrame(f"../../maps/environment/{output_path.name}", width="100%", height=height)
```

## Prepare tract geometry and metrics

```{python}
# Read the clean metrics with GEOID preserved as an 11-character string.
metrics = pd.read_csv(CLEAN_DATA_PATH, dtype={"GEOID": "string"})

# Read the 2020 tract polygons, normalize GEOID, and retain only the 31 CB3 rows.
tract_geometry = gpd.read_file(TRACT_GEOMETRY_PATH)
tract_geometry["GEOID"] = tract_geometry["geoid"].astype("string").str.zfill(11)
tract_geometry = tract_geometry[["GEOID", "geometry"]]

# Join the metrics to polygons and verify a complete one-to-one match.
tracts = tract_geometry.merge(
    metrics,
    on="GEOID",
    how="inner",
    validate="one_to_one",
)
tracts = gpd.GeoDataFrame(tracts, geometry="geometry", crs=tract_geometry.crs)

assert len(tracts) == 31
assert tracts["GEOID"].is_unique
assert set(tracts["GEOID"]) == set(metrics["GEOID"])

# Read tract-level demographics and join them to the same 31 tract polygons.
baseline_profile = pd.read_csv(BASELINE_PROFILE_PATH, dtype={"geoid": "string"})
baseline_profile["GEOID"] = baseline_profile["geoid"].str.zfill(11)

DEMOGRAPHIC_COLUMNS = [
    "GEOID",
    "median_household_income",
    "age_0_to_19_share",
    "age_20_to_64_share",
    "age_65_plus_share",
    "white_non_hispanic_share",
    "black_non_hispanic_share",
    "asian_non_hispanic_share",
    "hispanic_share",
    "poverty_rate",
    "lep_household_share",
]

tracts = tracts.merge(
    baseline_profile[DEMOGRAPHIC_COLUMNS],
    on="GEOID",
    how="left",
    validate="one_to_one",
)

# Read building-level rodent inspection points (native lat/lon, not collapsed
# to a tract centroid) and attach tract/NTA labels for tooltips.
rodent_insp_points = pd.read_csv(RODENT_INSPECTION_POINTS_PATH, dtype={"GEOID": "string"})
rodent_insp_points = rodent_insp_points.merge(
    tracts[["GEOID", "tract_label", "nta_name"]],
    on="GEOID",
    how="left",
    validate="many_to_one",
)

# Read individual living street tree points (native lat/lon) and attach
# tract/NTA labels for tooltips.
tree_points = pd.read_csv(TREE_POINTS_PATH, dtype={"GEOID": "string"})
tree_points = tree_points.merge(
    tracts[["GEOID", "tract_label", "nta_name"]],
    on="GEOID",
    how="left",
    validate="many_to_one",
)

# Read NYCHA residential building points (native lat/lon) and attach
# tract/NTA labels for tooltips. category is a constant column (all
# buildings render the same color) so add_categorical_point_layer's legend
# reads "NYCHA residential building" instead of listing 17 development names.
nycha_points = pd.read_csv(NYCHA_BUILDING_POINTS_PATH, dtype={"GEOID": "string"})
nycha_points = nycha_points.merge(
    tracts[["GEOID", "tract_label", "nta_name"]],
    on="GEOID",
    how="left",
    validate="many_to_one",
)
nycha_points["category"] = "NYCHA residential building"

# Read subsidized-housing building points (native lat/lon) from the Housing &
# Affordability domain and attach tract/NTA labels for tooltips. This file
# only covers buildings with a known 2025-2040 expiration window, not the
# full subsidized stock, so "at risk" here means "at-risk units with a known
# near-term expiration," not "all subsidized units."
subsidized_points = pd.read_csv(SUBSIDIZED_POINTS_PATH, dtype={"GEOID": "string"})
subsidized_points["at_risk_subsidized_units"] = (
    subsidized_points["subsidized_units_expiring_2025_2030"].fillna(0)
    + subsidized_points["subsidized_units_expiring_2031_2040"].fillna(0)
)
subsidized_points = subsidized_points.merge(
    tracts[["GEOID", "tract_label", "nta_name"]],
    on="GEOID",
    how="left",
    validate="many_to_one",
)
```

```{python}
# Build METRIC_SPECS and DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS from metric_metadata.yml.
# ej_area_flag is excluded here — it is rendered as a dedicated overlay, not a
# bubble layer, because it is binary and the concept doc calls for a hatched overlay.
def _to_map_spec(meta, include_filename=False):
    spec = {
        "dimension": meta["short_label"],
        "label":     meta["label"],
        "unit":      meta["unit"],
        "palette":   meta["palette"],
    }
    if include_filename:
        spec["filename"] = meta["filename"]
    for key in ("positive_values_only", "zero_color", "zero_label"):
        if key in meta:
            spec[key] = meta[key]
    return spec

# Tract-level environment metrics (excludes ej_area_flag, section-level
# metrics, and the non-tract layers below, which use category_colors instead
# of palette and are read directly by key rather than built into a spec dict).
METRIC_SPECS = {
    var: _to_map_spec(meta, include_filename=True)
    for var, meta in ALL_METRICS.items()
    if (
        meta["domain"] == "environment"
        and var != "ej_area_flag"
        and "filename" in meta
        and "palette" in meta
        and not meta.get("geography", "").startswith("DSNY")
    )
}

# Section-level sanitation metrics (DSNY cleaning sections, not census tracts).
SECTION_METRIC_SPECS = {
    var: _to_map_spec(meta, include_filename=True)
    for var, meta in ALL_METRICS.items()
    if meta.get("geography", "").startswith("DSNY") and "filename" in meta
}

# senior_poverty_rate is registered as a demographic metric for other domains'
# notebooks (e.g. seniors), but is not featured as a layer here.
EXCLUDED_DEMOGRAPHIC_METRICS = {"senior_poverty_rate"}

DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS = {
    var: _to_map_spec(meta)
    for var, meta in ALL_METRICS.items()
    if meta["domain"] == "demographics" and var not in EXCLUDED_DEMOGRAPHIC_METRICS
}

for metric in METRIC_SPECS:
    assert metric in tracts.columns, f"{metric} missing from tracts"
for metric in DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS:
    assert metric in tracts.columns, f"{metric} missing from tracts"

# Non-tract environment layers (grid cells, individual points/polygons) are
# registered in metric_metadata.yml too, but read directly by key at each
# map's construction site below rather than folded into METRIC_SPECS, since
# they don't share its numeric-quintile/tract-column shape.
for metric in ("pm25", "tree_health", "nycha_residential_building", "hurricane_evacuation_zone"):
    assert metric in ALL_METRICS, f"{metric} missing from metric_metadata.yml"
```

```{python}
def add_ej_overlay(map_object, show=True):
    """Add EJ-designated tracts as a dashed-border checkbox overlay.
    """
    ej_tracts = tracts[tracts["ej_area_flag"].eq(1)][["GEOID", "tract_label", "nta_name", "geometry"]].copy()
    folium.GeoJson(
        data=ej_tracts.to_json(),
        name="EJ Area (2024)",
        overlay=True,
        show=show,
        style_function=lambda _: {
            "fillColor": "#d95f02",
            "color": "#d95f02",
            "weight": 2,
            "dashArray": "5 4",
            "fillOpacity": 0.20,
        },
        highlight_function=lambda _: {
            "weight": 3,
            "fillOpacity": 0.35,
        },
        tooltip=folium.GeoJsonTooltip(
            fields=["tract_label", "nta_name"],
            aliases=["Census tract", "NTA"],
            sticky=False,
        ),
    ).add_to(map_object)
    add_zero_value_legend(
        map_object,
        "#d95f02",
        "Designated EJ Area (MOCEJ 2024)",
    )


# Thin, environment-domain-specific wrapper around build_metric_bubble_map,
# so call sites below don't have to repeat tracts/DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS/MAP_OUTPUT_DIR
# or re-add the EJ overlay on every call. Every other map-building function
# in this notebook (section, hurricane) routes through this one instead of
# calling build_metric_bubble_map directly, so the EJ overlay is added in
# exactly one place. metric_specs/title default to the tract-level METRIC_SPECS
# case; pass overrides for other geographies (DSNY sections) or ad hoc specs
# (the hurricane map's subsidized-housing bubble).
def create_single_metric_map(metric, metric_specs=None, title=None, extra_layers=None, **kwargs):
    metric_specs = metric_specs if metric_specs is not None else METRIC_SPECS
    spec = metric_specs[metric]

    # The EJ overlay is passed as build_metric_bubble_map's extra_layers hook
    # (composed with any caller-supplied extra_layers) so it's added before
    # that function's internal LayerControl, not after — a layer added after
    # LayerControl renders in a later script tag than the one that
    # references it, which throws a JS ReferenceError and silently breaks
    # the whole control.
    def combined_extra_layers(map_object):
        add_ej_overlay(map_object, show=False)
        if extra_layers is not None:
            extra_layers(map_object)

    return build_metric_bubble_map(
        tracts, metric_specs, DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS, metric,
        title=title if title is not None else f"CB3 Environmental Conditions: {spec['dimension']}",
        output_path=MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / spec["filename"],
        extra_layers=combined_extra_layers,
        **kwargs,
    )



```

## Separate maps


### Rodent inspections

```{python}
# Rodent inspections are reported at the building level, not the tract level,
# so bubbles use each building's own coordinates rather than a tract centroid.
rodent_map = create_single_metric_map(
    "rodent_active_inspections",
    points=rodent_insp_points,
    lat_col="latitude",
    lon_col="longitude",
    tooltip_fields=["address", "tract_label"],
    tooltip_aliases=["Address", "Census tract"],
)
show_map(MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / METRIC_SPECS["rodent_active_inspections"]["filename"])
```

### Street cleanliness (section level)

```{python}
# Load cleaning section geometry and averages for the section-level maps.
# The DSNY sections geojson uses the 'section' column (MN031–MN034), which
# matches the 'section' column in environment_sections.csv.
sections_geometry = gpd.read_file(SECTIONS_GEOMETRY_PATH)
sections_geometry = sections_geometry[sections_geometry["district"] == "MN03"][
    ["section", "geometry"]
].copy()
sections_geometry = sections_geometry.to_crs("EPSG:4326")

sections_metrics = pd.read_csv(SECTIONS_DATA_PATH)
assert len(sections_metrics) == 4, f"Expected 4 CB3 cleaning sections, got {len(sections_metrics)}"

# Attach each section's polygon centroid (sections have no native building-level
# coordinates), so cleanliness scores can be bubbled the same way as every
# other metric map instead of shown as a section-level choropleth.
section_points = add_polygon_centroids(
    sections_metrics, sections_geometry, "section",
    lat_col="section_centroid_latitude", lon_col="section_centroid_longitude",
)


def add_section_outline_overlay(map_object, show=True):
    """Add DSNY cleaning section boundaries as an unfilled checkbox overlay.

    Bubbles sit at each section's centroid, so the outline gives viewers the
    actual section boundary the bubble is standing in for.
    """
    layer_data = sections_geometry[["section", "geometry"]]
    folium.GeoJson(
        data=layer_data.to_json(),
        name="DSNY cleaning sections",
        overlay=True,
        show=show,
        style_function=lambda _: {
            "fillOpacity": 0,
            "color": "#3825a5",
            "weight": 3,
        },
        tooltip=folium.GeoJsonTooltip(
            fields=["section"],
            aliases=["Cleaning section"],
            sticky=False,
        ),
    ).add_to(map_object)


def create_section_map(metric):
    return create_single_metric_map(
        metric,
        metric_specs=SECTION_METRIC_SPECS,
        points=section_points,
        lat_col="section_centroid_latitude",
        lon_col="section_centroid_longitude",
        tooltip_fields=["section"],
        tooltip_aliases=["Cleaning section"],
        extra_layers=lambda m: add_section_outline_overlay(m, show=False),
    )
streets_map = create_section_map("avg_acceptable_streets_pct")
show_map(MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / SECTION_METRIC_SPECS["avg_acceptable_streets_pct"]["filename"])
```

### Sidewalk cleanliness (section level)

```{python}
sidewalks_map = create_section_map("avg_acceptable_sidewalks_pct")
show_map(MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / SECTION_METRIC_SPECS["avg_acceptable_sidewalks_pct"]["filename"])
```

### Street tree health

```{python}
# Living street trees are colored by health status rather than sized by
# magnitude, so each color is a fixed marker fill rather than a legend tied
# to bubble size. Colors and output filename are registered in
# metric_metadata.yml (category_colors) rather than hardcoded here.
TREE_HEALTH_COLORS = ALL_METRICS["tree_health"]["category_colors"]
TREE_HEALTH_OUTPUT_PATH = MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / ALL_METRICS["tree_health"]["filename"]


def create_tree_health_map():
    # No numeric metric to bubble here (each tree is a single point, not a
    # magnitude), so this is built directly from the generic layer helpers
    # rather than build_metric_bubble_map.
    map_object = make_base_map(tracts)
    add_demographic_backdrop_layers(map_object, tracts, DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS)
    add_ej_overlay(map_object, show=False)
    add_categorical_point_layer(
        map_object,
        tree_points,
        "health",
        TREE_HEALTH_COLORS,
        name="Street trees (health)",
        tooltip_fields=["spc_common", "address", "tract_label"],
        tooltip_aliases=["Species", "Address", "Census tract"],
        show=True,
    )
    add_zero_value_legend(map_object, "#d9d9d9", "Demographic data not available")
    add_map_title(
        map_object,
        "CB3 Environmental Conditions: Street Tree Health",
        "Choropleth: use the layer control to pick a demographic fill. "
        "Points: living street trees (2015 TreesCount census), colored by health status.",
    )
    folium.LayerControl(collapsed=False, position="topright").add_to(map_object)
    map_object.save(TREE_HEALTH_OUTPUT_PATH)
    return map_object

# Living street trees have no magnitude to bubble by, so each tree is plotted
# at its own coordinates as a fixed-size marker colored by health status.
tree_health_map = create_tree_health_map()
show_map(TREE_HEALTH_OUTPUT_PATH)
```

### Hurricane evacuation zones & at-risk housing

```{python}
# Hurricane evacuation zones are already clipped to the CB3 tract boundary in
# environment.py (the raw citywide file is 7MB covering the whole coastline).
evac_zones_clipped = gpd.read_file(EVAC_ZONES_PATH)
evac_zones_clipped["evacuation_zone"] = evac_zones_clipped["evacuation_zone"].astype(str)

# Zone 1 evacuates first (highest risk); "X" means not in an evacuation zone.
# Colors and output filename are registered in metric_metadata.yml
# (category_colors), same as tree health, rather than hardcoded here.
EVAC_ZONE_COLORS = ALL_METRICS["hurricane_evacuation_zone"]["category_colors"]
NYCHA_COLORS = ALL_METRICS["nycha_residential_building"]["category_colors"]
SUBSIDIZED_BUBBLE_COLOR = "#08589e"
HURRICANE_MAP_OUTPUT_PATH = MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / ALL_METRICS["hurricane_evacuation_zone"]["filename"]


def add_evacuation_zone_layer(map_object, show=True, legend_bottom_offset=35):
    """Add NYC Hurricane Evacuation Zones (clipped to CB3) as an always-on
    categorical polygon overlay, colored by zone severity. Unlike the
    demographic layers, zones aren't meant to be toggled one at a time —
    the map is read as one combined zone picture, same as NYC's own
    evacuation zone finder.
    """
    layer_data = evac_zones_clipped[["evacuation_zone", "geometry"]]
    folium.GeoJson(
        data=layer_data.to_json(),
        name="Hurricane evacuation zones",
        overlay=True,
        show=show,
        style_function=lambda feature: {
            "fillColor": EVAC_ZONE_COLORS.get(feature["properties"]["evacuation_zone"], "#d9d9d9"),
            "color": "#4d4d4d",
            "weight": 1,
            "fillOpacity": 0.55,
        },
        highlight_function=lambda _: {"weight": 3, "fillOpacity": 0.75},
        tooltip=folium.GeoJsonTooltip(
            fields=["evacuation_zone"],
            aliases=["Hurricane evacuation zone"],
            sticky=False,
        ),
    ).add_to(map_object)
    add_categorical_legend(
        map_object, "Hurricane evacuation zone", EVAC_ZONE_COLORS,
        bottom_offset=legend_bottom_offset, shape="square",
    )

# Ad hoc spec for create_single_metric_map: subsidized housing at risk is the
# one magnitude-sized layer on this map, so it doubles as the primary bubble
# metric; the evacuation zones (categorical polygons) and NYCHA buildings
# (no magnitude) are added via extra_layers instead of needing a bespoke map
# function. filename feeds create_single_metric_map's output_path.
HURRICANE_SUBSIDIZED_SPEC = {
    "dimension": "Subsidized housing at risk",
    "label": "Subsidized units at risk of expiring (2025-2040)",
    "unit": " units",
    "filename": HURRICANE_MAP_OUTPUT_PATH.name,
}


def create_hurricane_evacuation_map():
    # Legend offsets are staggered (35 / 260 / 460) so the subsidized-housing
    # bubble-size legend (up to 3 rows), the zone legend (up to 8 rows), and
    # the NYCHA point legend (1 row) stack without overlapping.
    def extra_layers(map_object):
        add_evacuation_zone_layer(map_object, show=True, legend_bottom_offset=260)
        add_categorical_point_layer(
            map_object,
            nycha_points,
            "category",
            NYCHA_COLORS,
            name="NYCHA residential buildings",
            tooltip_fields=["development_name", "address", "tract_label"],
            tooltip_aliases=["Development", "Address", "Census tract"],
            show=True,
            legend_bottom_offset=460,
        )

    return create_single_metric_map(
        "at_risk_subsidized_units",
        metric_specs={"at_risk_subsidized_units": HURRICANE_SUBSIDIZED_SPEC},
        title="CB3 Environmental Conditions: Hurricane Evacuation Zones & At-Risk Housing",
        points=subsidized_points,
        lat_col="latitude",
        lon_col="longitude",
        tooltip_fields=["standard_address", "tract_label"],
        tooltip_aliases=["Address", "Census tract"],
        color=SUBSIDIZED_BUBBLE_COLOR,
        extra_layers=extra_layers,
        subtitle=(
            "Choropleth: use the layer control to pick a demographic fill. Polygons: NYC hurricane evacuation "
            "zones (Zone 1 evacuates first). Bubbles: subsidized units with a known 2025-2040 expiration window "
            "(not the full subsidized stock). Points: NYCHA residential buildings."
        ),
    )

# NYC Hurricane Evacuation Zones layered over CB3, cross-overlaid with
# subsidized housing at risk of expiring and NYCHA residential buildings, to
# show how much regulated affordable stock sits in higher-risk zones.
hurricane_map = create_hurricane_evacuation_map()
show_map(HURRICANE_MAP_OUTPUT_PATH)
```


### Fine particulate matter (PM2.5)

```{python}
# NYCCAS PM2.5 is a modeled 300m-resolution surface, not a tract statistic
# (vectorized to one square polygon per grid cell in environment.py). Rather
# than drawing the raw squares, plot each cell at its own centroid as a bubble
# sized by magnitude, matching this notebook's "every numeric metric is a
# bubble" convention (see add_polygon_centroids, used the same way for the
# tract-level metrics and DSNY cleaning sections). Label/unit/filename are
# registered in metric_metadata.yml rather than hardcoded here.
PM25_SPEC = ALL_METRICS["pm25"]
PM25_OUTPUT_PATH = MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / PM25_SPEC["filename"]

pm25_grid = gpd.read_file(PM25_GRID_PATH)
pm25_points = add_polygon_centroids(
    pm25_grid, pm25_grid, "cell_id",
    lat_col="cell_centroid_latitude", lon_col="cell_centroid_longitude",
)


def create_pm25_map():
    map_object = make_base_map(tracts)
    add_demographic_backdrop_layers(map_object, tracts, DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS)
    add_ej_overlay(map_object, show=False)
    add_bubble_layer(
        map_object,
        pm25_points,
        "pm25",
        PM25_SPEC["label"],
        PM25_SPEC["unit"],
        name="PM2.5 (NYCCAS, 300m grid)",
        lat_col="cell_centroid_latitude",
        lon_col="cell_centroid_longitude",
        tooltip_fields=[],
        tooltip_aliases=[],
        # PM2.5 varies only modestly (~6.5-10.6 ug/m3) and never approaches
        # zero, so the default zero-anchored scaling would bunch every bubble
        # near max_radius (see radius_for's docstring). Scale relative to the
        # observed min instead, so the least/most polluted cells actually
        # look different sizes.
        scale_from_min=True,
    )
    add_zero_value_legend(map_object, "#d9d9d9", "Demographic data not available")
    add_map_title(
        map_object,
        "CB3 Environmental Conditions: Fine Particulate Matter (PM2.5)",
        "Choropleth: use the layer control to pick a demographic fill. Bubbles: NYCCAS modeled "
        "annual average PM2.5, Dec 2023-Dec 2024, plotted at each native 300m cell's centroid "
        "(not a tract statistic). DOHMH: not comparable to regulatory or short-term localized "
        "monitoring.",
    )
    folium.LayerControl(collapsed=False, position="topright").add_to(map_object)
    map_object.save(PM25_OUTPUT_PATH)
    return map_object

# The 300m NYCCAS grid doesn't align with tract boundaries, so each cell is
# mapped at its own centroid rather than averaged into a tract value.
pm25_map = create_pm25_map()
show_map(PM25_OUTPUT_PATH)
```

## Output validation

```{python}
expected_map_files = (
    [MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / spec["filename"] for spec in METRIC_SPECS.values()]
    + [MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / spec["filename"] for spec in SECTION_METRIC_SPECS.values()]
    + [TREE_HEALTH_OUTPUT_PATH]
    + [HURRICANE_MAP_OUTPUT_PATH]
    + [PM25_OUTPUT_PATH]
)

for map_file in expected_map_files:
    assert map_file.exists()
    assert map_file.stat().st_size > 10_000

print(f"Wrote {len(expected_map_files)} map files to {MAP_OUTPUT_DIR}")
```
